annotations.text: The fact that therapy with carbamazepine had been recently initiated focuses the differential diagnosis on cutaneous reactions to drugs.

Toxic epidermal necrolysis is thought to be a cell-mediated immunologic reaction to drug metabolites, which may accumulate because of abnormal metabolism of the drug, resulting in the persistence of immunogenic metabolites.
The extent of involvement and the clinical history in this patient are virtually diagnostic of this disorder.
Most deaths in patients with toxic epidermal necrolysis are a result of sepsis-induced organ failure.
The syndrome generally begins, as it did in this child, with a diffuse macular rash that spreads and becomes confluent, with separation of the epidermis, fluid loss, and bleeding
The diagnosis of toxic epidermal necrolysis can be confirmed by a skin biopsy, although one was not performed in this case.
It has been theorized that intravenous immune globulin may improve outcomes, given its salutary effect in another inflammatory condition, Kawasaki's disease.
The necrosis appears to be mediated by CD8+ T cells by way of Fas-mediated apoptosis.
Management of this condition requires strategies for wound care and for control of organ failure.
This child was admitted to the burn unit with a diagnosis of toxic epidermal necrolysis, with massive necrosis of the skin, rapidly progressive multiple-organ failure, and polymicrobial sepsis.
The denuded cornea and globe are very painful and prone to desiccation, corneal ulceration, and destructive local infection.
